summary: German Renaissance engraver and painter. Known for three 'master prints' (Melencolia I, St. Jerome in His Study, Knight Death and the Devil), the Rhinoceros woodcut, nature studies (Young Hare, Great Piece of Turf), and a Christ-like self-portrait at age 28. Also created The Four Apostles and Feast of the Rosary.

works.name: Self-Portrait at Twenty-Eight (1500)
works.description: Frontal self-portrait with Christlike iconography. Dürer grasps his fur robe, with flowing hair, monogram and date 1500. In the Alte Pinakothek. Conrad Celtes's secretary composed the Latin inscription. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Frontal self-portrait at age 28 with Christlike iconography — flowing hair, fur-trimmed coat, monogram and date 1500
examples: portrays its subject frontally with the artist's monogram and the date 1500 left of their long flowing hair and fur collar
controversially adopted the iconography of Christ for a 1500 self-portrait in which he holds his hand up to his chest

clues.clue: Latin inscription composed by Conrad Celtes's secretary mentions 'coloribus aetatis'; inscription says 'painted himself with indelible colors'
examples: an inscription composed by Conrad Celtes' secretary
flanked by his initials and an inscription saying that he 'painted himself with indelible colors'

clues.clue: Robert Mapplethorpe's photograph of Patti Smith imitates this self-portrait
examples: A Robert Mapplethorpe photograph simply titled 'Patti Smith' imitates a self-portrait by this artist

works.name: Melencolia I (1514)
works.description: Master print featuring a brooding winged figure with a magic square and hourglass above. One of three 'Meisterstiche' (master prints). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Master print: brooding winged figure with a magic square, hourglass, polyhedron, and other objects; 1514
examples: positioned a magic square and an hourglass above a brooding, winged figure in a 1514 'master print'
An angel praying next to a polyhedron
The title allegorical figure of this engraving is a brooding woman with angel wings, who sits beside a building inscribed with a four-by-four magic square

clues.clue: Truncated rhombohedron ('Dürer's solid') with faintly visible skull; emaciated dog; putto scribbling on chipped grindstone; comet/rainbow; bat holding banner with title
examples: A truncated rhombohedron in this work is known as its artist's namesake solid
an emaciated dog curls up next to an upturned, chipped millstone, upon which an ugly putto sits scribbling on a tablet
a bat unveils a banner with this work's title
a comet radiates light that creates a rainbow

clues.clue: Panofsky argued the figure represents one who can imagine Platonic forms but cannot create them; connected to Cornelius Agrippa's study
examples: Erwin Panofsky argued that this figure represents one who can imagine Platonic forms but cannot create them in the world in an analysis that connects her to a study by Cornelius Agrippa
One art historian claimed that objects in this work derived from an allegory in Gregor Reisch's Margarita philosophica

clues.clue: Keys hang from figure's belt; wreath of buttercup and watercress; compass on lap; clyster (enema syringe) half-hidden
examples: Keys hang from this figure's belt, and she wears a wreath of buttercup and watercress
Scholars debate whether a half-hidden object in the bottom right of this artwork is an enema syringe or a part of a bellows

clues.clue: Caspar David Friedrich drew on this work; Gunther Grass compared the figure to a tourist in From the Diary of a Snail
examples: Caspar David Friedrich drew on this work to create a depiction of a woman sitting below a spiderweb between two trees
Gunther Grass compared this figure to a tourist on a 'sightseeing conveyor built'

works.name: Saint Jerome in His Study (1514)
works.description: Master print: haloed saint writes the Vulgate at a desk; lion and dog sleeping on the floor; skull on windowsill. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Master print: haloed saint transcribes the Vulgate; lion and dog sleeping on the floor; skull on windowsill
examples: a lion slumbers in the foreground as the title saint transcribes the Vulgate
skull on the windowsill and a lion and dog sleeping on the floor as the title haloed man writes

works.name: Knight, Death, and the Devil (1513)
works.description: Master print: an armored knight on horseback rides past skeletal Death and a horned Devil. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Armored knight on horseback; skeletal Death figure (wreathed with snakes, holding hourglass) and goat-headed Devil alongside; a lizard runs beneath

this hourglass-carrying figure, a knight, and the Devil

clues.clue: Plaque reading 'S. 1513' on tree stump echoing Death; fox-tail may reference Erasmus's Handbook of a Christian Soldier
examples: the phrase anno salutis is signified by a plaque reading 'S. 1513' below an object echoing this figure
may have been inspired by Erasmus's Handbook of a Christian Soldier

works.name: Rhinoceros (1515)
works.description: Woodcut of an Indian rhinoceros based on first-hand accounts from Lisbon — Dürer never saw the animal. Body resembles plate armor. First such image in Europe since the 3rd century. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Woodcut of a rhinoceros based on accounts from Lisbon; Dürer never saw the animal; body resembles plate armor
examples: drew on first-hand accounts from Lisbon, despite never seeing the title creature himself, for a sketch showing the title Indian animal with a body resembling plate armor
The artist never saw the subject of this work, which was the first of its kind in Europe since the 3rd century

clues.clue: Originally given to Afonso de Albuquerque by Sultan Muzaffar Shah II; sent as a gift from Manuel I of Portugal to Pope Leo X
examples: originally given to Afonso de Albuquerque by Sultan Muzaffar Shah II and was later sent as a gift from Manuel I of Portugal to Pope Leo X

works.name: Nature Studies (Young Hare, Great Piece of Turf, etc.)
works.description: Highly detailed naturalistic watercolors including Young Hare (in the Albertina), a stag beetle, and The Great Piece of Turf.

examples: The Getty Museum pairs this artist's quote that 'art is omnipresent in nature' with his drawing of a stag beetle

works.name: The Four Apostles (1526)
works.description: Diptych with passages from Martin Luther's Bible translation. Currently in the Alte Pinakothek.

comprehensive_summary:
Albrecht Dürer (1471-1528) was the preeminent German Renaissance engraver and painter, based in Nuremberg where his godfather Anton Koberger ran the printing industry. He was apprenticed to Michael Wolgemut and contributed illustrations to Hartmann Schedel's Nuremberg Chronicle. He married Agnes Frey (called 'the old crow') and sent a lost self-portrait in gouache on cambric to Raphael. He added a lake in his Landauer Altarpiece as homage to Albrecht Altdorfer.

His three 'master prints' (Meisterstiche) are: Melencolia I (1514), featuring a brooding winged figure with a magic square, hourglass, truncated rhombohedron ('Dürer's solid' with faintly visible skull), emaciated dog, putto scribbling on a chipped grindstone, comet creating a rainbow, and a bat holding a banner with the title. Keys hang from the figure's belt; she wears a wreath of buttercup and watercress and holds a compass on her lap. Erwin Panofsky argued the figure represents one who can imagine Platonic forms but cannot create them, connecting it to Cornelius Agrippa's study. Caspar David Friedrich drew on it, and Gunther Grass discussed it in From the Diary of a Snail. Saint Jerome in His Study (1514) shows the haloed saint writing the Vulgate with a lion and dog sleeping on the floor and a skull on the windowsill. Knight, Death, and the Devil (1513) depicts an armored knight riding past skeletal Death (wreathed with snakes, holding an hourglass, with a plaque reading 'S. 1513') and a goat-headed Devil; a fox-tail may reference Erasmus's Handbook of a Christian Soldier.

His Self-Portrait at Twenty-Eight (1500) is a frontal portrait with Christlike iconography, flowing hair, fur-trimmed coat, and his monogram with the date 1500. The Latin inscription was composed by Conrad Celtes's secretary. Robert Mapplethorpe's photograph of Patti Smith imitates it.

His Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse woodcut, from a series on the Book of Revelation, depicts Death on an emaciated horse with a trident. He created the Feast of the Rosary (Maximilian I crowned by the Virgin Mary), the Large Triumphal Carriage and Triumphal Arch woodcuts for Maximilian I, and collaborated with Matthias Grunewald on the Heller Altarpiece. His Praying Hands drawing on blue paper may have been a study for the Heller Altarpiece — Andy Warhol made a blotted line copy that inspired Warhol's gravestone; Samuel Beckett was obsessed with the image. He created a witches engraving (skull at feet of four naked witches) and a woodcut of a witch riding a goat. His Rhinoceros (1515) woodcut is based on accounts from Lisbon — he never saw the animal, whose body resembles plate armor. His nature studies include Young Hare (Albertina), a stag beetle (Getty), and The Great Piece of Turf. The Four Apostles is a diptych with Luther's Bible translation, at the Alte Pinakothek. Milton Glaser was inspired by Dürer's watercolor of a European Roller.
